title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing a new organic supermarket that has opened in their area.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Have you been to that new 'Green-Earth' market yet? Woman: I popped in yesterday, actually. [1]The layout is beautiful, but the prices are eye-watering[/1]. I mean, four pounds for a punnet of strawberries? Man: I know, it’s a bit steep. But they are locally sourced and completely pesticide-free. You’re paying for the quality, I suppose. Woman: I’m all for supporting local farmers, but I can't justify doing my weekly shop there. [2]It feels more like a boutique than a supermarket[/2]. Man: True, it’s definitely targeted at a certain demographic. But I must say, the sourdough I bought was the best I've ever had. Woman: Oh, their bakery section is dangerous! I went in for milk and came out with a gourmet hamper. Man: That’s how they get you. Everything looks so appetising you end up overspending without realizing it.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Have you been to that new 'Green-Earth' market yet?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I popped in yesterday, actually. The layout is beautiful, but the prices are eye-watering. I mean, four pounds for a punnet of strawberries?

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I know, it’s a bit steep. But they are locally sourced and completely pesticide-free. You’re paying for the quality, I suppose.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: True, it’s definitely targeted at a certain demographic. But I must say, the sourdough I bought was the best I've ever had.

dialogue.speaker: woman

dialogue.speaker: man

questions.question: What was the woman's first impression of the shop?

options.A: She was impressed by the wide variety of products.
options.B: She was put off by the excessive cost of items.
options.C: She found the store's organization confusing.

questions.explanation: She describes the prices as 'eye-watering', which is a C1 idiom for extremely expensive.

questions.question: The woman compares the supermarket to a 'boutique' to suggest that...

options.A: it is too small for her needs.
options.B: it lacks the range of a standard grocery store.
options.C: it prioritises style and exclusivity over practicality.

questions.explanation: Calling a supermarket a 'boutique' implies it's more about a high-end experience/aesthetic than being a practical place for a weekly shop.

extracts.context: You hear two coworkers, a man and a woman, talking about a colleague who has just retired.
extracts.audio_script: Man: It’s going to be strange not having Arthur around the office anymore, isn't it? Woman: Definitely. He was like the institutional memory of this place. [3]Whenever there was a technical glitch that nobody could solve, he’d have it fixed in minutes[/3]. Man: I know. He had such a wealth of experience. Though I won't miss his endless stories about 'how things were in the seventies'. Woman: [4]Oh, I found those quite charming! It gave us a bit of perspective on how much the industry has evolved[/4]. Man: I guess. But sometimes I just wanted to get my coffee and go back to my desk without a twenty-minute history lesson. Woman: You’re so impatient! Anyway, the retirement party was a nice touch. I think he was genuinely moved by the gift. Man: He was. Though I’m not sure he’ll actually use that digital camera we got him. He’s a bit of a technophobe outside of work.

dialogue.text: It’s going to be strange not having Arthur around the office anymore, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Definitely. He was like the institutional memory of this place. Whenever there was a technical glitch that nobody could solve, he’d have it fixed in minutes.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I know. He had such a wealth of experience. Though I won't miss his endless stories about 'how things were in the seventies'.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Oh, I found those quite charming! It gave us a bit of perspective on how much the industry has evolved.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I guess. But sometimes I just wanted to get my coffee and go back to my desk without a twenty-minute history lesson.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: You’re so impatient! Anyway, the retirement party was a nice touch. I think he was genuinely moved by the gift.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: He was. Though I’m not sure he’ll actually use that digital camera we got him. He’s a bit of a technophobe outside of work.

questions.question: The woman highlights Arthur's ability to...
questions.explanation: She says he'd have technical glitches 'fixed in minutes' when nobody else could solve them.

questions.question: How did the woman feel about Arthur's anecdotes?

options.A: She found them a waste of valuable working time.
options.B: She appreciated the historical context they provided.
options.C: She felt they were often repetitive and dull.

questions.explanation: She calls them 'charming' and says they gave 'perspective on how much the industry has evolved'.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing their approach to planning a holiday.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: I’ve finally booked the flights for our trip to Japan! Now I just need to map out every single day's itinerary. Man: Every day? [5]Can't we just leave some things to chance?[/5] Part of the fun is discovering things spontaneously. Woman: In a country where we don't speak the language? I’d rather have a plan and not need it than be stuck in a train station with no idea where to go. Man: I'm not saying we shouldn't have a rough idea, but [6]scheduling every hour feels more like a military operation than a holiday[/6]. Woman: It’s not about being rigid; it’s about efficiency. There’s so much to see, and I don't want to waste time debating where to eat when we're already starving. Man: I see your point, but I think there’s a middle ground. How about we book the main attractions and leave the afternoons free? Woman: I suppose I could live with that. As long as we have a list of potential options for the 'free' time.

dialogue.text: I’ve finally booked the flights for our trip to Japan! Now I just need to map out every single day's itinerary.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Every day? Can't we just leave some things to chance? Part of the fun is discovering things spontaneously.

dialogue.speaker: woman

questions.question: The man expresses a preference for...

options.C: visiting fewer places in more detail.

questions.explanation: He asks 'Can't we just leave some things to chance?' and talks about 'discovering things spontaneously'.

questions.question: What is the man's main objection to a detailed itinerary?

options.A: It would be too expensive to execute.
options.B: It takes away the relaxation of a holiday.
options.C: It is impossible to follow in a country like Japan.

questions.explanation: He compares it to a 'military operation', implying it's too structured and stressful for a holiday.
